---
title: "Neuroinfectious Disease"
---

NEUROINFECTIOUS DISEASES
MENINGITIS vs. ENCEPHALITIS (start treatment empirically, don’t wait for LP…):
Meningitis Encephalitis
S/S: HA, photo/phono/meningismus, N/V, fever, S/S: similar to meningitis, plus acute confusion,
malaise, irritability personality changes, seizures
Bugs: Bugs: HSV-1 (#1), arbo (SLE, LAC, EEE, WEE),
Viral: echo, coxsackie, mumps, HSV-2, adeno WNV, VZV, legionella
Bacterial: by age…
<1m: GBS, E.coli, Listeria (“hear bells”)
1-23m: S.pneumo, N.mening, H.flu, E.coli
2-50y: N.mening, S.pneumo
50+y: S.pneumo, N.mening, Listeria, GNR
W/U: HCT (cid:31) LP (routine, HSV PCR, t/c W/U: HCT (cid:31) if no abscess (cid:31) LP (routine, HSV PCR,
crypto/TB/AFB/viral studies) t/c viral/toxo) (cid:31) t/c MRI and EEG
Treatment: empirically… Treatment: by bug…
Viral: supportive (if high suspicion for HSV, start HSV/VZV: acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8 x 14+d
acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8) CMV: ganciclovir or foscarnet
Bacterial: <1m cefotax/ceftriax + ampicillin Legionella: azithro, quinolone, tatracyclines
1-23m cefotax or ceftriax + vanco
2-50y ceftriax + vanco
50+y ceftriax + vanco+ ampicillin
Fungal: amphoB + flucytosine + fluconazole
TB: rifampin+INH+pyrazinamide+ethambutol,
+/- streptomycin (“RIPES”)
* if trauma: use cefepime or meropenem
* Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days was shown
to be beneficial in subgroups with pneumococcal
meningitis, 1st dose 20 min before abx.
Prognosis: Prognosis:
- aseptic/viral: most improve over 2 weeks HSV& EEE = 70% die untreated, 25% treated
- bacterial: 20% mortality (S. pneumo worst) Others = 5-20% mortality rate
Tunkel AR et. al. Practice guidelines for the management of bacterial meningitis. Clin Infect Dis 2004 Nov
1;39(9):1267-84.
de Gans et. Al. Dexamethasone in adults with bacterial meningitis. NEJM, 2002; 347(20): 1549-56.
Serum Studies
1. Viruses: Measure IgM and IgG antibodies acute and convalescent (4 fold rise in IgG): St. Louis, West Nile,
Eastern and Western Equine, Japanese encephalitis, Dengue fever
EBV (VCA IgM and IgG and EBNA), VZV, HSV-1 (IgM), Rabies, HIV (RNA can be checked early when viral
load high but ELISA weakly positive or negative)
2. Tick-borne bacteria: IgG & IgM by indirect immunofluorescence for RMSF,
Lyme ELISA- confirmatory Western blot, Ehrlichia antibodies by indirect fluorescent antibody
3. Parasites:
Toxoplasma gondii
- No IgG or IgM Abs does not exclude toxoplasma encephalitis in AIDS patients
- If +ve in HIV patient, tx with pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine and folinic acid.
(Incidence reduced among people receiving prophylaxis with sulfadiazine or dapsone and pyrimethamine against
Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia)
b) If toxoplasma serology negative, check CSF EBV pcr and consider brain biopsy of any accessible mass lesion
for lymphoma
Taenia solium (cysticercosis): ELISA, but 50% with solitary lesions will be seronegative
